{{Short description|Electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths shorter than ultraviolet}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = X-ray
| image = Röntgen-Röhre.svg
| caption = Diagram of an [[X-ray tube]], producing X-rays by accelerating electrons to strike a metal target.
| topics = [[Electromagnetic wave]], [[Electromagnetic spectrum]], [[Photon]]
| wavelength = 0.01–10 nm
| frequency = 30 PHz – 30 EHz
| energy = 100 eV – 100 keV
}}

'''X-rays''' (or '''Röntgen radiation''') are a form of high-energy [[electromagnetic radiation]] with [[wavelength]]s ranging from about 10 [[picometre]]s to 10 [[nanometre]]s, corresponding to [[frequency|frequencies]] in the range 30 [[petahertz]] to 30 [[exahertz]] and [[photon]] energies in the range 100 eV to 100 keV.

X-rays were discovered by [[Wilhelm Röntgen]] in 1895, for which he received the first [[Nobel Prize in Physics]] in 1901.

== Production ==
X-rays are produced by two mechanisms:

=== Bremsstrahlung ===
When high-speed [[electron]]s are decelerated upon striking a metal target, they emit [[bremsstrahlung]] (braking radiation) X-rays with a continuous spectrum.

=== Characteristic X-rays ===
When an inner-shell [[electron]] is ejected from an [[atom]] and an outer-shell electron falls in to fill the vacancy, a [[photon]] of energy equal to the energy difference is emitted — a ''characteristic X-ray'' of that element.

== Properties ==
* Travel at the [[speed of light]] in vacuum
* Can penetrate materials opaque to [[visible light]]
* Can [[ionizing radiation|ionize]] atoms and damage biological tissue
* Exhibit [[diffraction]], [[interference (wave propagation)|interference]], and [[polarization (waves)|polarization]]

== Applications ==
* [[Radiography]] — medical imaging (bones, teeth, chest)
* [[CT scan]] — three-dimensional medical imaging
* [[X-ray crystallography]] — determining crystal and molecular structures (DNA, proteins)
* [[X-ray astronomy]] — observing high-energy celestial objects
* [[X-ray fluorescence]] — elemental analysis
* [[Airport security]] — baggage scanning
* [[Industrial radiography]] — weld and structural inspection

== Bragg's law ==
{{Main|Bragg's law}}
X-ray diffraction by crystals follows [[Bragg's law]]:
:<math>n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta</math>
where <math>d</math> is the spacing between crystal planes.

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Bragg's law]]
* [[Electromagnetic spectrum]]
* [[Electromagnetic wave]]
* [[Gamma ray]]
* [[Photon]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:X-rays| ]]
[[Category:Electromagnetic radiation]]
[[Category:Medical imaging]]
[[Category:Ionizing radiation]]
